tossups.question: A house designed by this man includes two symmetrical wings leading out to pavilions that sport sundials underneath the pediments of their façades. After his death, a theater designed by this man was given a trompe-l’œil set designed to look like a long street heading upstage. A church designed by this man uses white marble for a four-column, elevated classical temple façade that’s superimposed over a second, lower temple façade. A country house designed by this man has (*) porticos facing each of the four cardinal directions. This designer of the Olympic Theater adapted Roman temple designs for his many villas in and around Vicenza. Monticello was built on principles inspired by this designer of San Giorgio Maggiore. For 10 points, name this Venetian Renaissance architect who wrote the Four Books on Architecture and designed the Villa Rotonda.
tossups.question_sanitized: A house designed by this man includes two symmetrical wings leading out to pavilions that sport sundials underneath the pediments of their facades. After his death, a theater designed by this man was given a trompe-l'œil set designed to look like a long street heading upstage. A church designed by this man uses white marble for a four-column, elevated classical temple facade that's superimposed over a second, lower temple facade. A country house designed by this man has (*) porticos facing each of the four cardinal directions. This designer of the Olympic Theater adapted Roman temple designs for his many villas in and around Vicenza. Monticello was built on principles inspired by this designer of San Giorgio Maggiore. For 10 points, name this Venetian Renaissance architect who wrote the Four Books on Architecture and designed the Villa Rotonda.

bonuses.leadin: The priest Lucius Caecilius Metellus lost his eyesight trying to save a statue that the Romans held to be identical to this object. For 10 points each:
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: The priest Lucius Caecilius Metellus lost his eyesight trying to save a statue that the Romans held to be identical to this object. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this sacred object that Diomedes and Odysseus steal from Troy to dispel its protective influence over the city.
The Palladium is a sacred Trojan statue of this goddess of war and wisdom.

An Athenian king of this name took the Palladium from Diomedes after overcoming him in a nighttime battle. A young prince of this name was the son of Metaneira, who shrieked when she saw a ritual to which he was subjected.
parts_sanitized: Name this sacred object that Diomedes and Odysseus steal from Troy to dispel its protective influence over the city.
The Palladium is a sacred Trojan statue of this goddess of war and wisdom.

An Athenian king of this name took the Palladium from Diomedes after overcoming him in a nighttime battle. A young prince of this name was the son of Metaneira, who shrieked when she saw a ritual to which he was subjected.

bonuses.leadin: For a “basilica” in this city, an architect active there designed a series of tripartite, arched exteriors for its loggia that have become “windows” alternatively named for that architect or Sebastiano Serlio. For 10 points each:
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: For a "basilica" in this city, an architect active there designed a series of tripartite, arched exteriors for its loggia that have become "windows" alternatively named for that architect or Sebastiano Serlio. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this city home to a theater completed by Vincenzo Scamozzi, whose scenery uses trompe l’oeil (“tromp LOY”) to depict streets receding from its stage. That theater’s original architect met his mentor Gian Giorgio Trissino in this city.
The Teatro Olimpico, and many other buildings in Vicenza, were designed by this architect. Thomas Jefferson’s design for Monticello was inspired by this architect’s Villa La Rotonda.

Palladio is called the “architect of Vicenza” in Daniele Barbaro’s introduction to this man’s writings, which Palladio illustrated. His writings inspired Leonardo’s drawing of a man with ideal body proportions.
parts_sanitized: Name this city home to a theater completed by Vincenzo Scamozzi, whose scenery uses trompe l'oeil ("tromp LOY") to depict streets receding from its stage. That theater's original architect met his mentor Gian Giorgio Trissino in this city.
The Teatro Olimpico, and many other buildings in Vicenza, were designed by this architect. Thomas Jefferson's design for Monticello was inspired by this architect's Villa La Rotonda.

Palladio is called the "architect of Vicenza" in Daniele Barbaro's introduction to this man's writings, which Palladio illustrated. His writings inspired Leonardo's drawing of a man with ideal body proportions.
answers_sanitized: Andrea Palladio [or Andrea di Pietro della Gondola]

bonuses.leadin: An object of this name was washed in the yearly Plynteria and was said to have fallen from the sky in Athens. For 10 points each:
answers: Palladium [or Palladion; do not accept or prompt on "Pallas"]
parts: Give the name of that cult statue of Athena. Troy was legendarily protected by a wooden statue of the same name, which was made by Athena to depict a companion whose name she took as an epithet.

The Palladion was housed in this temple which was dedicated to both Athena and Poseidon and, like the Parthenon, was built under Pericles' leadership. It is named for a king of Athens who was identified with Poseidon.
Near the Erechtheum was a shrine to this Greek god, who used an axe to split open Zeus's head so Athena could be born. He serves as the gods' blacksmith.
answers_sanitized: Palladium [or Palladion; do not accept or prompt on "Pallas"]
Erechtheum [or Erechtheion] (It is named for Erechtheus.)
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: An object of this name was washed in the yearly Plynteria and was said to have fallen from the sky in Athens. For 10 points each:
parts_sanitized: Give the name of that cult statue of Athena. Troy was legendarily protected by a wooden statue of the same name, which was made by Athena to depict a companion whose name she took as an epithet.

The Palladion was housed in this temple which was dedicated to both Athena and Poseidon and, like the Parthenon, was built under Pericles' leadership. It is named for a king of Athens who was identified with Poseidon.
Near the Erechtheum was a shrine to this Greek god, who used an axe to split open Zeus's head so Athena could be born. He serves as the gods' blacksmith.

bonuses.leadin: Answer some questions about the Roman calendar, for 10 points each:
parts: This month, named after instruments of purification, was the last month of the original Roman calendar. During it, the festivals of Parentalia, to honour ancestors, and Lupercalia, noted for its fertility-inducing foot race, were held.

This god, not shared with the Greek pantheon, was worshipped as the god of beginnings and endings. The doors of this two-headed god’s temple were kept open during times of war – and so were hardly ever closed.

This wooden object, a statue of Athena, was believed to protect the city of Rome, despite its noted failure to protect Troy. Lucius Caecilius Metellus, Pontifex Maximus, was reportedly blinded while rescuing this object when the Temple of Vesta burnt down in 241 BCE.
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: Answer some questions about the Roman calendar, for 10 points each:
parts_sanitized: This month, named after instruments of purification, was the last month of the original Roman calendar. During it, the festivals of Parentalia, to honour ancestors, and Lupercalia, noted for its fertility-inducing foot race, were held.

This god, not shared with the Greek pantheon, was worshipped as the god of beginnings and endings. The doors of this two-headed god's temple were kept open during times of war - and so were hardly ever closed.

This wooden object, a statue of Athena, was believed to protect the city of Rome, despite its noted failure to protect Troy. Lucius Caecilius Metellus, Pontifex Maximus, was reportedly blinded while rescuing this object when the Temple of Vesta burnt down in 241 BCE.

bonuses.leadin: This white stone mansion on the Brenta Riviera has a raised front portico that can be accessed through twin side stone stairways. For 10 points each:
parts: Identify this building that is also known as La Malcontenta, due to the legend that the spouse of its owner was locked up there when she refused to sleep with him.
The Villa Foscari was designed by this Italian architect of the Villa Barbaro whose classically-derived style influenced Thomas Jefferson.
The Villa Foscari is located a few miles west of this city, which contains Palladio’s monumental churches Il Redentore and San Giorgio Maggiore.
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: This white stone mansion on the Brenta Riviera has a raised front portico that can be accessed through twin side stone stairways. For 10 points each:
parts_sanitized: Identify this building that is also known as La Malcontenta, due to the legend that the spouse of its owner was locked up there when she refused to sleep with him.
The Villa Foscari was designed by this Italian architect of the Villa Barbaro whose classically-derived style influenced Thomas Jefferson.
The Villa Foscari is located a few miles west of this city, which contains Palladio's monumental churches Il Redentore and San Giorgio Maggiore.

bonuses.leadin: The façade of this building was completed by Vincenzo Scamozzi, working on a design from another architect. For 10 points each:
parts: Identify this building, whose white, temple-like façade was designed to be seen from the city across a gap of water. Tintoretto's diagonal Last Supper is located in this building.

This architect of the Villa Rotonda died before his work on San Giorgio Maggiore was complete. Thomas Jefferson heavily plagiarized this author of the Four Books of Architecture in buildings such as Monticello.

This Canadian-American architect wrote a monograph about Palladio titled The Perfect House. This onetime architecture critic for Slate also wrote Home: A Short History of an Idea and a microhistory of the screwdriver titled One Good Turn.
answers: Church of San Giorgio Maggiore [or Basilica of San Giorgio Maggiore]
answers_sanitized: Church of San Giorgio Maggiore [or Basilica of San Giorgio Maggiore]
Andrea Palladio [or Andrea Di Pietro della Gondola]
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: The facade of this building was completed by Vincenzo Scamozzi, working on a design from another architect. For 10 points each:
parts_sanitized: Identify this building, whose white, temple-like facade was designed to be seen from the city across a gap of water. Tintoretto's diagonal Last Supper is located in this building.

bonuses.leadin: The first chief residents of this building are depicted in statue form as Caesar and his wife; those statues decorate the sarcophagus of a chapel later added to this mansion by William Kent. For 10 points each:
parts: Identify this country house designed in Woodstock, England, by then-novice architect John Vanbrugh, for the Duke of Marlborough.

Blenheim Palace mostly avoided a popular style of English architecture named for this Italian, whose treatise The Four Books of Architecture was so influential that Thomas Jefferson abided by it for Monticello.

The popularity of the Palladian style in the British isles was further undermined by the elegant style invented by this Scottish architect of the Kenwood House and Osterley Park. He took into account furniture and other interior objects in his designs.

Blenheim Palace mostly avoided a popular style of English architecture named for this Italian, whose treatise The Four Books of Architecture was so influential that Thomas Jefferson abided by it for Monticello.

The popularity of the Palladian style in the British isles was further undermined by the elegant style invented by this Scottish architect of the Kenwood House and Osterley Park. He took into account furniture and other interior objects in his designs.

bonuses.leadin: Identify these authors of architectural tracts, for 10 points each:
parts: This Italian, much admired by Thomas Jefferson, designed the Villa Rotunda according to principles laid out in his Four Books of Architecture.
This man updated Vitrivuis's De architectura in the ten books of his De Re Aedificatoria. This polymath also wrote On Painting and developed a namesake cypher in his De Cifris.

This author of Spoken into the Void designed Prague's Villa Muller and a namesake bank building in Vienna, though he's best known for his 1908 work excoriating the Vienna Secession, Ornament and Crime.
answers: Andrea Palladio or Andrea di Pietro della Gondola
answers_sanitized: Andrea Palladio or Andrea di Pietro della Gondola

This man updated Vitrivuis's De architectura in the ten books of his De Re Aedificatoria. This polymath also wrote On Painting and developed a namesake cypher in his De Cifris.

This author of Spoken into the Void designed Prague's Villa Muller and a namesake bank building in Vienna, though he's best known for his 1908 work excoriating the Vienna Secession, Ornament and Crime.
